attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic requesting preconception counselling. She has type 1 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 12 and takes insulin via an insulin pump. Her most recent glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c) is 8.2% (66 mmol/mol). She has background retinopathy but no other complications. Her other medications include folic acid 5 mg daily. She works as an accountant and does not smoke or drink alcohol.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 24 kg/m2.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely increased risk to her future pregnancy?
attributes.explanation:
This woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus is seeking preconception counselling. Poor glycaemic control in early pregnancy significantly increases the risk of congenital anomalies. Her current HbA1c of 8.2% indicates suboptimal control that should be improved before conception.

Congenital heart disease is the most common congenital anomaly associated with maternal diabetes, with a 3-5 fold increased risk compared to the general population. Common cardiac defects include ventricular septal defects, transposition of the great arteries, and truncus arteriosus.

Neural tube defects are associated with maternal diabetes, but the risk is lower than that of cardiac defects. Additionally, this patient is already taking high-dose folic acid which helps reduce this risk.

Cleft palate has a slightly increased risk in diabetic pregnancies but occurs less frequently than cardiac defects.

Renal agenesis can occur in diabetic pregnancies but is less common than cardiac defects.

Limb reduction defects are rare complications that can occur in poorly controlled diabetes but are less frequent than cardiac anomalies.

The key to reducing the risk of all congenital anomalies is achieving optimal glycaemic control before conception and maintaining it throughout pregnancy. The target HbA1c for pregnancy is less than 6.5% (48 mmol/mol) if this can be achieved safely without significant hypoglycaemia.
learningPoints: Women with type 1 diabetes mellitus have a 3-5 fold increased risk of having a baby with congenital heart disease.
Preconception counselling and optimisation of glycaemic control is essential to reduce the risk of congenital anomalies in diabetic pregnancies.
First trimester HbA1c >7.0% is associated with increased risk of congenital anomalies.
references: Lemaitre M, Bourdon G, Bruandet A, Lenne X, Subtil D, Rakza T, Vambergue A. Pre-gestational diabetes and the risk of congenital heart defects in the offspring: A French nationwide study. Diabetes Metab. 2023 Jul;49(4):101446. doi: 10.1016/j.diabet.2023.101446. Epub 2023 Apr 7. PMID: 37031733.
